SUMMARY:

![VSPARTICLE](/fileadmin/_processed_/c/4/csm_logo_r_green-8_a763fc76c5.png)

## Nanoporous marvels | Introduction to nanoporous materials and their application in electrocatalysis and gas sensing technologies

Nanoporous materials or layers refer to substances with structures characterized by a network of pores or voids at the nanometer scale, typically ranging from 1 to 100 nm in size. The nanoporous nature of these materials provides an exceptionally high surface area relative to their volume, which is a key factor in their utility. This heightened surface area allows for increased interactions with other substances, such as gases, liquids, or molecules, making nanoporous materials valuable in a range of applications.

According to the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC), nanoporous materials can also be classified based on pore size: microporous material (pore sizes from 0 to 2 nm, mesoporous material (2 to 50 nm), and macroporous materials (greater than 50 nm). These nanoscale pores endow the materials with unique properties and functionalities, making them versatile for various applications across scientific, technological, and industrial domains.

Nanoporous materials can be found in various forms, including films, membranes, powders, and bulk structures. They can be engineered to have specific pore sizes, shapes, and distributions, allowing for tailoring of their properties to suit desired applications. The ability to manipulate and engineer nanoporous materials opens up exciting avenues for innovation, enabling advancements in diverse scientific disciplines and technological applications.

#### A plethora of application areas

Over the last decade, there has been an ever-increasing interest and research effort in the synthesis, characterization, functionalization, and design of nanoporous materials. Research efforts in this field have been driven by the rapidly growing emerging applications such as biosensors, drug delivery, gas separation, energy storage, fuel cell technology, and catalysis. These applications offer exciting new opportunities for scientists to develop new techniques for the synthesis and applications of these materials.

Nanoporous materials demonstrate remarkable versatility with impactful applications, notably in the realms of green hydrogen production and gas sensing.

### The pressing need for sustainable energy solutions

![bulb_electrocatalysis](/fileadmin/_processed_/b/0/csm_bulb_electrocatalysis_238617ec63.png)

Energy is at the heart of the climate challenge. Fossil-fuel dominated electricity generation in the United States and China has enormous environmental consequences. In 2007, 2.4 billion metric tons of carbon dioxide (CO2) were emitted from electricity generation in the United States, about 40 percent of the country’s energy-related greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. In the same year, electricity generation in China produced just over 2 billion metric tons of CO2, accounting for about one-third of its energy-related GHG emissions. [1]

This scenario underscores the pressing need to reassess and transition from conventional energy sources to more sustainable alternatives. In this context, the concept of green hydrogen emerges as a promising solution to address the environmental impact of energy production.

Nanoporous materials demonstrate remarkable versatility with impactful applications, especially in the field of green hydrogen production. As a matter of fact, many literature studies already indicate that nanoporous materials offer promising performances in catalyzing and optimizing electrocatalytic processes (such as water electrolysis, CO2 reduction to hydrocarbons etc., etc.) due to their enhanced surface area/ surface energy.

Although the physical and chemical properties in the nano-scale sizes are still largely unexplored, it is already widely assumed that nanoporous materials could be advantageous to improve the thermodynamics of electrochemical reactions at the electrode/electrolyte interface, as well as to promote a better electron transfer. Moreover, the enhanced surface area exposed during an electrochemical reaction provides more active sites and higher mass diffusion capability, which could definitely have a positive impact on its electrochemical performances.

The significance of nanoporous materials lies in their ability to act as highly active electrocatalysts. The performance of heterogeneous catalysts strictly depends on their structures and physicochemical properties. Heterogeneous catalysts with large specific surface areas possess more catalytic sites to enhance the overall catalytic performance. Nanoporous materials emerged as highly active heterogeneous catalysts due to their large internal surface area, enabling a high density of active catalytic sites. The presence of nanopores also allows the selectivity towards the desired products.[2]

The ability of nanoporous materials to enhance catalytic efficiency hugely contributes to the advancement of sustainable and eco-friendly green hydrogen production, a crucial component in the global shift towards renewable energy.

### Nanoporous materials in gas sensing technologies

![gas_sensors1](/fileadmin/_processed_/9/3/csm_gas_sensors1.1_318f4f9c0c.jpg)

Gas sensing technologies are indispensable for safety, environmental monitoring, and industrial optimization. These sensors contribute to workplace safety by detecting hazardous gas concentrations, ensuring air quality through pollution monitoring, and enhancing industrial processes by optimizing combustion and controlling production. They play a vital role in early fire detection, support energy efficiency efforts by minimizing waste, find applications in medical diagnostics, aid environmental research, and are increasingly integrated into smart home systems for enhanced safety.

In 2020, household air pollution led to approximately 3.2 million deaths, with more than 237,000 fatalities among children under 5 years old. The combined impact of both ambient and household air pollution contributes to around 6.7 million premature deaths each year. Urgent measures, such as the widespread adoption of clean fuels and technologies, are necessary to mitigate household air pollution and safeguard public health. [3]

The current gas sensing technologies are mainly governed by metal-oxides (MOXs) based gas sensors as a result of their gas sensing performances and fabrication methods. For instance, these materials have high sensitivity towards a wide range of gases, fast response and recovery time, high thermal and mechanical stabilities, are easy to miniaturize, and there are relatively low costs involved in their production [4].

However, the major drawback of this sensor technology is its poor selectivity towards certain target analytes [5]. The selectivity of a gas sensor is a paramount performance metric of a sensing device, basically related to the ability to detect a target gas in the presence of other gases. The poor selectivity dramatically affects the reliability of the sensor because the device cannot accurately detect the presence of a specific gas concentration due to its cross-sensitivity with other gases surrounding the sensing device.

In addition to selectivity constraints, the next generation of gas sensors will need to fulfill new energetic requirements to excel in the future Internet of Things (IoT) era. The MOX-based gas sensors are considered to have a low power consumption compared with other commercially available sensors [6]. Nevertheless, the fact that their operation temperature is at least 200 °C can affect the power consumption budget given the massive network of sensors that will need to continuously acquire, analyze and share data [7].

In recent decades, many efforts have been made to outperform the current gas-sensing technology. Nanoporous materials are attractive solutions due to their tuneable structural, thermal, optical, and electronic properties. Besides, their large surface-to-volume ratio significantly increases the active sites interacting with a target analyte, compared with bulk counterparts' sensors. The nanoporous layers can be engineered in many ways, such as creating heterostructures, junctions, and doping the material. Collectively, these properties render the nanoporous materials an essential building block for the conception of the next generation of gas sensors that can reduce the challenges associated with their performance, fabrication, and implementation.

### The imperative of developing accurate nanoporous materials

Research and development on technologies to create accurate nanoporous layers are vital for unlocking the full potential of these materials across a spectrum of applications. From catalysis to gas sensing and energy storage, the tailored engineering of nanoporous layers is essential for realizing advanced functionalities that contribute to sustainable and innovative technological solutions. The ongoing pursuit of these technologies is key to addressing pressing global challenges and driving the transition towards a cleaner and more efficient future.

## Introducing VSParticle's innovative nano printing technology

VSParticle is a pioneer in the field of nanoparticle generation and printing. The company’s flagship product, VSP-P1, enables a fast and easy synthesis and printing of (semi-) conductive nanoporous layers for various applications.

![CCM_zoom](/fileadmin/_processed_/b/6/csm_CCM_zoom_1b2afe6b17.jpg)

The versatility of the nanoprinters developed by VSParticle is also another aspect that can be exploited in the catalyst manufacturing process. In fact, when the VSP-P1 nanoprinter is coupled with multiple nanoparticles G1 generators, many unexplored materials can be created in a relatively short time, for example, high entropy materials (HEM) with five or more metals in equiatomic or near atomic ratios. Recent studies also showed that many of these materials possess outstanding properties due to (I) a high number of active sites, (II) elemental diversity, (III) optimal interaction between catalyst surface and reaction intermediates. In light of these properties, HEM have also been utilized for water electrolysis, showing superior activity and stability performances with respect to the conventional low-entropy materials.

VSParticle also aims to occupy the field of high-throughput experimentation (HTE), namely a technique that aims to test simultaneously a large number of experiments in a relatively short time. In fact, one of the biggest challenges in electrocatalysis is the exploration of novel electrocatalysts, wherein the nano printer developed by VSParticle is expected to have a great impact on science. The capability to produce a diverse range of nanoporous materials with high reproducibility represents the promise to further boost catalyst discovery in many fields of electrocatalysis, from water electrolysis to oxygen evolution reaction. This will be beneficial for the entire scientific community in order to enrich the existing catalyst libraries and improve the current state—of—the—art of many other electrochemical reactions in a relatively short time.

For more information about VSParticle’s technology and explore the possibilities for your field, please get in touch with us.

Abstract
--------

The development of nanoporous materials has gained significant attention due to their unique structural properties and multimodalities, which are highly relevant for advanced sensing technologies. The capability to directly grow nanoporous materials on flexible substrates or indirectly integrate them into soft templates through mixing and dispersion opens exciting opportunities for a new class of flexible and stretchable electronics for personalized healthcare applications. This review paper provides a snapshot of recent advancements in flexible nanoporous materials and their applications, emphasizing biological and biomedical sensors. The review highlights the material of choice for flexible and stretchable substrates and effective approaches to synthesize and integrate nanoporous architectures onto soft polymers. Applications from wearable sweat sensors, mechanical sensors for electronic skins, implantable bioelectronics, and gas sensing are also presented. The paper concludes with current challenges and future perspectives within this highly active research paradigm.

1. Introduction
---------------

Flexible electronics are among the most active and rapidly growing research areas in the last decade, particularly in wearable and implantable technology, attributed to the breakthroughs in materials science and advancement in soft lithography [[[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0005), [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0010), [[3]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0015), [[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0020), [[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0025), [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0030)]. Traditional sensors face limitations due to their rigid nature, which impedes effective analyte collection due to the mechanical mismatching with the area of contact, resulting in low signal quality and undesirable artifacts. In contrast, flexible sensors with the unique capability to conform to uneven surfaces can efficiently capture target analytes and produce more reliable biopotential measurements, particularly physiological signals such as electromyogram (EMG), electrocardiography (ECG), and electroencephalogram (EEG) [[[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0035), [[8]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0040), [[9]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0045)]. The progression of flexible devices is closely linked to innovative materials engineering strategies encompassing active, nanoarchitectured materials that are conductive and multifunctional, along with the development of patternable flexible substrates [[[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0050), [[11]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0055), [[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0060)]. In this regard, incorporating nanoporous materials with large surface areas on flexible substrates facilitates a pathway towards the significant augmentation of sensor efficacy. Nanoporous materials, with typical pore sizes of less than 100 nm, represent a heterogeneous group of compounds distinguished by their pore structures at the nanometer scale, offering several unique physical and chemical characteristics owing to their high surface area-to-volume ratio, tunable pore sizes, and specific surface geometries [[[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0065), [[14]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0070), [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0075), [[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0080)]. These materials can be systematically developed and categorized based on several criteria: pore size, pore structure, crystallinity, and material type. As per the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC), porous materials are categorized based on their pore size into microporous (less than 2 nm), mesoporous (approximately 2–50 nm), and macroporous (greater than 50 nm) [[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0085)]. Owing to their exceptionally higher surface area and distinctive physicochemical characteristics, these nanoporous materials have attracted considerable interest in the realms of catalysis [[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0090)], energy storage [[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0095),[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0100)], and actuation [[[21]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0105), [[22]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0110), [[23]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0115)].

The field of nanoporous materials is expansive and rapidly evolving, offering substantial opportunities for cross-disciplinary research in sensing. In particular, large surface areas facilitate mass diffusion through interconnected channels and high pore diameters, ensuring rapid reaction and recovery times by enhancing sensitivity and response time [[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0120)]. Their high surface-to-volume ratio increases active sites for target analyte interaction, outperforming bulk material sensors. In addition, selective absorption based on pore sizes or solubility enables the detection and differentiation of specific analytes. These features have been utilized in various sensing applications such as inorganic sensing [[25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0125)], anion detection [[26](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0130)], colorimetric and fluorometric chemical sensing [[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0135),[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0140)], biosensing [[[29]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0145), [[30]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0150), [[31]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0155)], gas sensing [[32](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0160),[33](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0165)], electrochemical analysis [[34](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0170),[35](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0175)], and antibiotic agent sensing [[36](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0180)]. The engineering process of nanoporous materials involves controlling parameters such as porosity, pore diameters, and surface functionality, which significantly impact their adsorption capacity, catalytic activity, and selectivity towards different molecules (i.e., gases and toxic elements). Engineering these parameters can enhance the performance of sensors for medical diagnostics, gas monitoring, biosecurity, and pharmaceutical development [[[37]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0185), [[38]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0190), [[39]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0195), [[40]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0200)]. Together with the significant progress in bottom-up synthesis processes, the advancement in top-down manufacturing technologies has enabled the integration of nanoporous materials into well-defined microstructures for functional sensors. Various cost-effective, time-efficient, and scalable manufacturing techniques have been established to develop a broad range of nanoporous devices in the form of portable, wearable, and implantable systems for different types of biological sensing applications.

This review highlights recent advances in flexible nanoporous materials (with pore sizes between 1 and 100 nm) and devices, covering the physical properties and manufacturing approaches for soft substrates ([section 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#s0010)) and several synthesis and growth techniques for nanoporous architectures ([section 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#s0015)). While porous architectures cover a broad range of pore sizes. The paper describes different design concepts and sensing principles for chemical, mechanical, and physiological sensing devices tailoring large surface areas offered by the high density of nanopore networks ([section 4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#s0065)), as indicated in [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#f0005). The review concludes with existing challenges and potential research directions for the future development of multimodal sensors employing this class of nanoarchitectures.

Fig. 1. Overview of flexible nanoporous materials for diverse sensing applications.

2. Flexible polymeric substrates for nanoporous sensors
-------------------------------------------------------

To enable the use of sensors in wearable and implantable biosensing scenarios, it is imperative that the functioning and constructing elements exhibit a high degree of flexibility or deformability to enable a conformal interface with the contours of the human skin [[41](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0205)]. In this regard, selecting suitable soft polymeric substrates that allow for the growth of nanoporous materials and simultaneously fabricating relevant microstructures for seamless device integration into human tissues is critically important. Several studies have been conducted to explore suitable polymeric substrates that meet requirements in mechanical properties and manufacturing capability and for flexible sensors.

Non-stretchable polymers, such as PET [[42](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0210)] and PI [[43](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0215)], are known for their high optical transparency, low flexural rigidity, excellent chemical inertness, and biocompatibility [[44](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0220)] with Young's modulus of 9 GPa [[45](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0225)] and 1.6–23 GPa [[46](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0230),[47](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0235)], respectively, are preferred for skin adhesive sensors. These substrates also serve as ideal platforms for advanced fabrication techniques such as lithography ([Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#f0010)a), etching, deposition, printing, and laser machining to create complex microstructures [[48](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0240)]. Their chemical resistance allows them to withstand various solvents and etchants used during these processes, further enhancing their utility in multi-step fabrication workflows. For instance, structural engineering techniques have been employed to generate different geometric configurations to adapt to dynamic motions and deformation of human skin. These modifications include designing the polymer into wavy [[[49]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0245), [[50]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0250), [[51]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0255)], buckled, wrinkled [[52](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0260),[53](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0265)], serpentine [[54](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0270)], island [[55](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0275)], and net [[56](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0280)] configurations. Such geometric alterations allow the materials to accommodate mechanical deformation by stretching, bending, or compressing in a controlled manner, thereby enabling stretchability in sensors without inducing significant strain on the substrate material [[[53]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0265), [[54]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0270), [[55]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0275),[[57]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0285), [[58]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0290), [[59]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0295), [[60]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0300), [[61]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0305), [[62]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0310)]. The wavy structures allow the material to extend and compress as needed, while buckled and wrinkled configurations enable the material to absorb mechanical stress by deforming at the surface level without altering the underlying material properties [[63](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0315)]. Serpentine designs, characterized by their meandering pathways, provide high flexibility and stretchability by effectively distributing strain across the material. In the case of island structures, where rigid electronic components are placed on flexible substrates, they are connected by flexible interconnects that can stretch and bend, allowing the device to deform while maintaining the integrity of the electronic components. Net configurations offer a mesh-like structure that can expand and contract, further enhancing the material stretchability. Furthermore, these substrates can withstand severe environmental, chemical, and thermal conditions necessary for the operation of the device while retaining their mechanical flexibility [[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0055),[64](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0320),[65](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0325)]. While offering several advantages, some main drawbacks of these polymers include their poor breathability and limited stretchability, affecting their practicality for specific designs and applications that require shrinking.

Fig. 2. **The step-by-step process towards fabricating flexible nanoporous electrodes. a.** Illustration of flexible device fabrication using photolithography and deposition on non-stretchable substrate. Reprinted from [[48](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0240)]. Copyright (2020), with permission from Springer Nature. **b.** Illustration of the 3D printing process on intrinsically stretchable material. The fabrication procedure begins with the creation of an object using 3D printing technology to achieve microporosity. Subsequently, washing steps are employed to extract the PEG porogen, resulting in the formation of nanopores. In the images, the dark regions indicate the porous nature of the structures. Reproduced from [[78](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0390)]. Copyright (2024) with permission from Wiley-VCH GmbH.

To address the deficiency in stretchability exhibited by prevalent polymer plastic substrates like polyethylene terephthalate (PET), polyethylene naphthalate (PEN), and polyimide (PI), intrinsically stretchable substrates emerge as effective alternatives. Elastomeric polymers, including polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS), thermoplastic polyurethane (TPU), Ecoflex, styrene-ethylene/butene-styrene (SEBS), and polymer hydrogels, have proven to be effective stretchable substrates with low elastic moduli, high stretchability, optical transparency, biocompatibility, and high tensile strain [[[66]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0330), [[67]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0335), [[68]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0340), [[69]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0345), [[70]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0350), [[71]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0355)]. These properties enable significant deformation without risk of tearing and provide diverse surface attachment options on several body areas such as thighs, forearms, and joints. However, these intrinsically stretchable substrates present unique challenges in patterning due to their elastic and deformable nature. Unlike rigid or non-stretchable materials, these substrates do not easily lend themselves to conventional patterning techniques, particularly photolithography, which require precise control over dimensions and feature alignment [[49](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0245),[72](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0360)]. As a result, alternative fabrication methods, such as mold casting [[73](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0365),[74](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0370)] and 3D printing ([Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#f0010)b), are often employed to create micropatterns on these substrates [[[75]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0375), [[76]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0380), [[77]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0385), [[78]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0390)]. Meanwhile, stretchable devices can be realized through structural engineering by integrating non-stretchable, rigid electronic components onto an elastomeric substrate. This method combines stretchable interconnects with stiff island components, where the stretchable interconnects allow for flexibility and movement, while the stiff islands house the essential electronic components. This design strategy leverages the mechanical properties of elastomeric substrates, which can undergo significant deformation, to enable the overall device to bend and stretch.

In addition to these categories, microporous substrates such as sponge-like materials have also been employed as flexible substrates. A wide range of well-established sponge materials has been recognized for suitability for this role. These include TPU, TPU/epoxy composite sponges, and PI sponges. Additionally, innovative materials like poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) polystyrene sulfonate (PEDOT) coated melamine sponges, metal-coated PDMS sponges, as well as sponges coated with graphene or carbon black on polyurethane (PU) matrices, have been identified as highly effective templates for various applications [[[39]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0195), [[40]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0200), [[41]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0205), [[42]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0210), [[43]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0215), [[44]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0220), [[45]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0225), [[46]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0230)]. The unique properties of these materials, such as their flexibility, conductivity, and mechanical stability, make them ideal candidates for advanced applications in fields like energy conversion, sensing, and wearable electronics.

Despite the remarkable flexibility, polymer substrates are constrained by certain drawbacks, such as thermal and dimensional instability, with substantial shrinkage during heating and cooling cycles (e.g., for the development of flexible heaters for thermal therapy) [[79](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0395)]. It is essential for glass transition temperature (T g) to align with the device fabrication temperature, as prolonged exposure to temperatures beyond the threshold of polymer substrate leads to irreversible deformation and film disruption, consequently degrading the performance of the printed devices [[80](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001868625000508#bb0400)]. Many conventional wireless skin-interfaced bioelectronic devices utilize traditional thermoset silicone elastomer materials such as PDMS for soft e

enriched_points.expanded_content: Nanoporous materials enhance catalysis, sensing, and energy storage due to their high surface area, enabling innovations in electrocatalysis, gas sensing, and biomedical applications.
These materials play a pivotal role in green hydrogen production and environmental monitoring, facilitating eco-friendly solutions and reducing reliance on fossil fuels.
Ongoing research in nanoporous materials promises breakthroughs in flexible electronics, advanced biosensing, and sustainable energy solutions, driving the evolution of smart technologies.
